nativeRange.habitat: Wild base species (A. stuartgranti, A. jacobfreibergi, and related Aulonocara spp.) inhabit the sediment-sand interface zones and rocky-sand transition areas of Lake Malawi, typically along the lake floor where they hunt invertebrates using their lateral-line pit system. OB color-form individuals are captive-bred hybrids and do not occur in the wild.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (3–5 cm) for natural foraging behaviour
rock piles and caves along the back and sides (cichlid-stack style)
open sand swimming areas in the front
no dense planting required — Vallisneria or Amazon swords tolerated
good surface area and footprint more important than tank height

diet.notes: Primarily an invertivore in the wild, using a unique sensory pit system along the lower jaw to detect invertebrates buried in sand. In captivity the OB Peacock generally accepts high-quality cichlid pellets, Spirulina-enriched foods, and frozen or freeze-dried brine shrimp, mysis, daphnia, and bloodworms. Mammal-based proteins such as beef heart or whole liver are best avoided, as they have been linked to Malawi bloat in rift lake cichlids. One to two small meals daily is the commonly recommended feeding schedule.

goodWith: other Aulonocara peacock cichlids (mixed species peacock tank)
Copadichromis and other non-aggressive Utaka haplochromines
Sciaenochromis fryeri (Electric Blue Hap) — with space
Plecos and bottom dwellers that tolerate alkaline water
avoidWith: Mbuna (rock-dwelling cichlids — highly aggressive, will outcompete and harass)
small tetras, rasboras, or other community fish incompatible with rift lake water parameters
other dominant male Aulonocara in small tanks (reduce to 1 male per species per tank)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase image for Aulonocara stuartgranti (wild type, not OB form). Useful for species identification; OB trade fish will differ significantly in coloration.
Wikimedia Commons A. stuartgranti wild type (Flavescent Peacock). Shows base species body shape; OB forms are captive hybrid lines with orange blotch patterning on females.

summary: The OB Peacock is a captive-bred hybrid color form derived from Aulonocara stuartgranti. OB stands for Orange Blotch — a marbled orange-and-black pattern that appears primarily in females, while males typically display the vivid iridescent blue and orange coloration of the base species. It is well suited to Lake Malawi community tanks kept alongside other peaceful open-water cichlids.
careNotes:
The OB Peacock is widely considered one of the most popular Malawi peacock cichlids in the hobby, though the name describes a selectively bred or hybridised color morph rather than a distinct wild species. 'OB' stands for Orange Blotch — a color pattern originally found in female Metriaclima zebra, a rock-dwelling Mbuna cichlid, and introduced into the Aulonocara lineage through deliberate hybridisation, most commonly using A. stuartgranti or A. jacobfreibergi as the base species. The result is a line where females and occasional males display vivid marbled orange and black patterning; OB males are rare and prized because the OB gene, carried on the X chromosome, normally expresses only in females (which carry two X chromosomes). Males of OB lines typically display the standard brilliant iridescent blue, yellow, and orange coloration of the base peacock species.

Because OB Peacocks are hybrids, their exact scientific designation varies by breeder and lineage; A. stuartgranti is most commonly cited in the trade because it is the most frequently used base species, though some lines may incorporate A. jacobfreibergi or other Aulonocara. Aquarists interested in preserving wild Aulonocara genetics should seek out species-pure lines from specialist breeders.

Tank setup should replicate a Lake Malawi rocky-sand biotope: hard, alkaline water (pH 7.8–8.6, gH 10–20, kH 8–15, temperature 24–28°C), a fine sand substrate at least 3 cm deep for natural foraging behaviour, and rock stacks along the back and sides providing territory and retreat. A 200-litre tank with at least 120 cm footprint is the recommended minimum; larger is better for managing male aggression. One male per Aulonocara species is generally advised — multiple males of visually similar species tend to fight. A ratio of one male to three to five females per species is considered optimal. Malawi bloat — an internal bacterial or parasitic infection associated with high-protein mammal foods such as beef heart — is a known risk with rift lake cichlids, so the diet should centre on quality cichlid pellets and invertebrate-based frozen foods. Weekly water changes of 20–30% help maintain the buffering capacity and low nitrate levels that rift lake cichlids require.
breedingNotes: OB Peacock Cichlids are maternal mouthbrooders, meaning the female incubates the eggs inside her mouth rather than on a nest. Spawning takes place on open sand, where the male performs a 'dummy egg' display — presenting the egg-spot markings on his anal fin to attract the female. After depositing and fertilising 15–60 eggs, the female collects them orally and holds them for approximately 21–28 days at 26°C. Females should not be stripped early unless disease is a concern; allowing natural holding to at least day 18 produces the most developed fry. The female does not feed during mouthbrooding and may lose condition through multiple broods, so ensuring she can recover between spawns is important. Fry are released as free-swimming, fully formed juveniles capable of taking micro-pellets, finely crushed cichlid food, and brine shrimp nauplii immediately. A separate breeding or grow-out tank helps reduce fry predation. Sexing juveniles is generally difficult until males begin developing adult coloration at around 6–12 months. In OB lines, females that inherit the OB gene display the orange-blotch pattern; the rare OB male, which carries two copies of the OB allele, tends to command premium prices in the trade.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Peacock Cichlid Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary trade source. Covers tank size (55–75 gal / 208–284 L minimum for community setups), water parameters (pH 7.8–8.5, temperature 76–82°F / 24–28°C), diet (cichlid pellets, frozen invertebrate foods, avoid high-fat mammal proteins), tankmates (peaceful haplochromines, Synodontis; avoid Mbuna), and general peacock cichlid breeding overview. OB as a colour morph/hybrid lineage noted in trade context.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Aulonocara stuartgranti
additional.notes: Detailed wild-species profile for the base species A. stuartgranti. Confirms taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Lake Malawi, rocky/sandy transition zones), adult male size to 15 cm, water parameters (pH 7.6–8.8, hardness 6–25 dH, temperature 24–26°C), sand-foraging behaviour via lateral-line pit system, maternal mouthbrooding with 21–28 day incubation, and minimum tank 200 L. Notes that commercial 'OB Peacock' lines are hybridised with other Aulonocara and potentially Mbuna strains and do not represent a pure A. stuartgranti lineage.

additional.name: FishBase — Aulonocara stuartgranti
additional.notes: Confirms taxonomy, native range (Lake Malawi, Malawi and Mozambique), adult size (male to 15 cm TL), habitat (benthic, sandy-rocky margins, 3–20 m depth), diet (benthic invertebrates detected by lateral-line), and reproductive strategy (maternal mouthbrooder, substrate spawner). Wild-type species data used to anchor biological parameters for the OB hybrid line.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — OB Peacock Cichlid
additional.notes: Feature article on peacock cichlids including OB colour forms. Confirms OB as a hybrid trade strain where the orange-blotch gene — originally from Mbuna lineage — produces the marbled orange-black colouration predominantly in females; OB males are rare and result from both X chromosomes carrying the OB allele. Recommends keeping peacocks in haplochromine-only or peacock/hap community tanks and emphasises separating from Mbuna. Tank size 200+ L, 1 male per species, 3–5 females per male.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Understanding OB Cichlids
additional.notes: In-depth genetics piece on OB cichlids. Explains that 'OB' (Orange Blotch) is an X-linked dominant colour trait: females (XX) expressing OB are common; OB males (carrying OB allele on one or both X chromosomes relative to Y) are uncommon but occur in some lines. Details the history of OB introduction into the Aulonocara lineage from Metriaclima (Mbuna) hybridisation and discusses the hobbyist debate around OB Peacocks as hybrids vs. selectively bred lines. Recommends clear labelling in the trade to distinguish OB hybrids from wild-type Aulonocara.

fish.breedingNotes: OB Peacock Cichlids breed as maternal mouthbrooders: the female incubates 15–60 eggs orally for 21–28 days following spawning on a sand substrate. The male initiates courtship through an egg-spot display on his anal fin, which triggers the female to pick up the eggs after fertilisation. A separate breeding tank is recommended to protect both the holding female and the released fry. OB patterning in offspring follows X-linked inheritance, meaning OB females are common while OB males, which require two copies of the OB allele, are relatively rare.
